planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in late winter to early spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Big Red mayhaw fruits in May (hence the name 'May-haw') — harvest the small, red fruits from the ground or by shaking branches over tarps when the berries begin dropping naturally. Trees begin fruiting in 3–5 years from planting and become increasingly productive for decades. Mayhaw prefers wet, poorly drained, acidic soils — a wetland native that grows naturally in swamps, bayou edges, and bottomlands of the Gulf South. Excellent cross-pollinator: plant two or more varieties for maximum production.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist to wet, acidic to neutral soil — mayhaw is a wetland native that actually thrives in poorly drained areas where most fruit trees would fail; Big Red is an improved cultivar of native mayhaw (Crataegus aestivalis), the regional fruit of the Gulf South that has been gathered wild for generations and more recently cultivated for commercial jelly production. The small (grape to crabapple sized), round, bright red berries are borne in clusters and ripen in May — giving the tree its common name — with a tart, mildly astringent, aromatic flavor that makes exceptional jelly and is used in sauces, wines, and sweet preparations throughout southern Louisiana, Mississippi, and east Texas where mayhaw culture is a regional food tradition. Mayhaw jelly is the Gulf South equivalent of beach plum jelly in New England — a hyperlocal, regionally specific preserve made from a native fruit with no close cultural equivalent elsewhere in the United States. Big Red was selected for larger-than-average fruit size and high productivity compared to wild-collected mayhaw. The tree's unusual preference for wet, acidic, swampy conditions makes it one of the few commercially valuable fruit trees suitable for low-lying zone 9 sites that would be unsuitable for conventional orchards.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Mayhaw Production and Culture
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Mayhaw for Gulf Coast
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Crataegus aestivalis Native Plant

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in late winter to early spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Illinois Everbearing is one of the most cold-hardy mulberry cultivars and produces fruit over an exceptionally long season (6–8 weeks vs 2–3 weeks for single-crop varieties). Trees are fast-growing, reaching significant size (30–35 feet) quickly — allow ample permanent space. The fruit stains everything it contacts — plant away from walkways, patios, and vehicles. In zone 9 mulberries produce prolifically in April–May and may fruit again in fall.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — mulberry is adaptable to a wide range of soil types and conditions; Illinois Everbearing is a hybrid mulberry (Morus alba x Morus rubra) that produces the longest fruit season of any widely available mulberry cultivar — harvesting over 6–8 weeks from late spring into early summer compared to the brief 2–3 week window of standard mulberry varieties. The large, cylindrical, 1–1.5 inch fruits ripen from red through dark purple-black over the extended season, with the sweet, mild, slightly bland flavor characteristic of mulberries — less complex than blackberries, softer-textured, and milder-tasting, primarily valued for fresh eating, smoothies, and baked goods where the abundant, easily harvested fruit compensates for the relatively mild flavor. The practical appeal of Illinois Everbearing is unambiguous: a single established tree produces quantities of fruit (hundreds of pounds in a mature tree) over an extended period with essentially zero cultivation requirements beyond harvesting. As a wildlife plant, mulberry is unparalleled — the fruit is consumed by more than 60 species of birds and dozens of mammal species — making it the single most productive wildlife-supporting tree available for home landscapes.
sources: University of Illinois Extension — Illinois Everbearing Mulberry
Oklahoma State University Extension — Mulberry Production Guide
University of Georgia Extension — Mulberry for Southern Home Orchards

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter (dormant) or container-grown trees in late winter to early spring; in zone 9 plant January–February. Elliot pecan requires 1,500–2,000 hours below 45°F for optimum production — easily met in zones 6–9. Plant two varieties for cross-pollination (pecans are wind-pollinated and most cultivars benefit from another variety nearby). Elliot is a Type II variety — releases pollen after female flowers are receptive; pair with a Type I variety (Stuart, Cape Fear) for best production. Trees become significantly productive in 7–10 years and can remain productive for 100+ years.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep (6+ feet), well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — pecans are deep-rooted trees that require deep, fertile bottomland or well-amended upland soil for maximum production; Elliot is one of the most important pecan cultivars for the Gulf South, selected specifically for its outstanding resistance to pecan scab (Venturia effusa) — the most damaging fungal disease of pecans in the humid Gulf Coast climate where without resistance, pecan production requires intensive fungicide programs that make orchard production impractical. Elliot's scab resistance means it can produce quality nuts in the humid Gulf South with minimal or no fungicide applications — a fundamental practical advantage for home orchard growers. The small-to-medium, ovate nuts have thin shells and a high kernel percentage (approximately 52%), with the sweet, rich, buttery, distinctively pecan flavor that makes Gulf South-grown pecans the gold standard among nut crops. Elliot's consistent production, excellent kernel quality, and scab resistance make it the recommended home orchard pecan for Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, and the Texas Gulf Coast — the regions where pecan is culturally important and where Elliot's disease resistance matters most.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pecan Variety Selection for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Home Pecan Production
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Pecan Variety Trials

planting.note: Plant container-grown or rooted cutting transplants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Excel figs begin producing in their second year and become increasingly productive as trees establish. In zone 9 figs typically produce two crops: a small breba (early) crop on the previous season's wood in June–July, and the main crop on new wood in August–October. Excel is well-adapted to the Gulf South climate and produces good crops even in partial shade. Prune lightly in late winter to shape the tree and remove dead wood; heavy pruning reduces fruit production.

directions: Plant in full sun to light partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — figs are adaptable to a wide range of soils but require good drainage; Excel is an LSU (Louisiana State University) AgCenter-developed fig cultivar released specifically for Gulf South production, selected for its large, distinctively yellow-skinned fruit with rich amber flesh and the high resistance to fruit souring that is a serious problem for figs in the humid Gulf Coast climate. While traditional Southern figs like Celeste and Brown Turkey are prone to splitting and souring in rainy, humid conditions at harvest time, Excel was specifically bred for improved resistance to these problems — producing firmer skin that resists splitting and reduces the entry of yeasts that cause souring. The large, sweet fruits have an extremely rich, complex, honey-fig flavor that is the archetypal fresh fig experience — one of the most sophisticated and complex flavors of any temperate fruit. Figs are the quintessential Gulf South home fruit tree: tolerant of heat, humidity, and occasional drought once established, self-fruitful (no cross-pollination required), highly productive, and producing fruit of exceptional quality that cannot be found in stores (fresh figs ship and store very poorly, making the homegrown experience irreplaceable).
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Excel Fig Variety Release
LSU AgCenter — Fig Production in Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Fig Culture for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–April. Brown Select is among the earliest-ripening of all satsuma mandarins — ripening in late September to October, 3–4 weeks earlier than Owari Satsuma. This earliness is its key distinguishing advantage, providing fresh homegrown citrus before any other commonly grown mandarin ripens. Cold hardiness is comparable to Owari (to approximately 15°F when fully dormant). Trees begin producing their first significant crop in the third or fourth year and reach full production in 6–8 years. Protect young trees with frost cloth during the first 2–3 winters.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil — satsuma mandarins require excellent drainage and regular deep watering during the growing season; Brown Select is a distinctly early-ripening Satsuma mandarin developed and selected in Louisiana as an improvement on Owari Satsuma in terms of harvest timing — the trees produce ripe, full-flavored, easy-peeling mandarins in late September to October, making Brown Select the first commonly grown citrus to ripen in the Gulf South citrus season. The flavor at peak ripeness is comparable to Owari — sweet, aromatic, with the characteristic Satsuma complexity — but the earlier timing extends the homegrown citrus season by 3–4 weeks before Owari becomes ready. Brown Select carries the same exceptional cold hardiness as Owari (among the hardiest of all citrus to freezing temperatures) and the same practical advantage of loose, easily peeled skin and nearly seedless, juicy flesh. The combination of extreme cold hardiness, high productivity, and early ripening makes Brown Select specifically important to Gulf South citrus culture in the zones where winter cold is a genuine risk factor for citrus growing, and where starting the fresh fruit season as early as possible is valuable.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Brown Select Satsuma Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Satsuma Citrus for Gulf Coast
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Early-Ripening Citrus Varieties
